audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 24, first-pass draft aligned later; compare 1 Chronicles 21
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for conflicting incitement traditions, census as military and extractive administration, Joab's objection, route and totals, David's conscience, three punishments and variants, ruler-chosen collective suffering, plague deaths, Divine relenting, angel imagery, David's command responsibility, Araunah's Jebusite land and power imbalance, payment variants, animal sacrifice, and plague cessation.

verses.restored: Anger of YHWH again burned against Israel, and YHWH incited David against them, saying, Go, count Israel and Judah.
King said to Joab, commander of army who was with him, Go about through all tribes of Israel, from Dan to Beersheba, and muster/count people, that I may know number of people.
Joab said to king, May YHWH your Divine add to people, however many they are, one hundred times, while eyes of my lord king see. But why does my lord king delight in this thing?
Word of king prevailed against Joab and commanders of army. Joab and commanders went out from before king to count people of Israel.
They crossed Jordan and camped at Aroer, on right side of city that is in middle of wadi of Gad, toward Jazer.
They came to Gilead and land of Tahtim-hodshi; they came to Dan-jaan and around toward Sidon.
They came to fortress of Tyre and all cities of Hivites and Canaanites, then went out to Negev of Judah at Beersheba.
They went about through all land and came to Jerusalem at end of nine months and twenty days.
Joab gave number of census of people to king: Israel was eight hundred thousand valiant men drawing sword, and men of Judah were five hundred thousand.
David's heart struck him after he counted people. David said to YHWH, I have sinned greatly in what I did. Now, YHWH, please cause iniquity of your servant to pass, because I acted very foolishly.
When David arose in morning, word of YHWH came to prophet Gad, seer of David, saying,
Go and speak to David: Thus says YHWH, Three things I am laying before you; choose one of them, and I will do it to you.

Gad came to David, told him, and said: Shall seven years/three years of famine come to you in your land, or will you flee three months before your adversaries while they pursue you, or shall there be three days of plague in your land? Now know and see what word I shall return to one who sent me.
David said to Gad, I am greatly distressed. Let us fall into hand of YHWH, because YHWH's mercies are many, but let me not fall into hand of humanity.
YHWH gave plague in Israel from morning until appointed time. From people, from Dan to Beersheba, seventy thousand men died.

Angel stretched hand toward Jerusalem to destroy it, but YHWH relented concerning evil and said to angel destroying among people, Enough; now release your hand. Angel of YHWH was by threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

David spoke to YHWH when he saw angel striking people and said, Look, I sinned and I acted perversely, but these sheep—what did they do? Please let your hand be against me and against house of my father.
Gad came to David that day and said to him, Go up; raise for YHWH an altar at threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.
David went up according to word of Gad, as YHWH commanded.
Araunah looked and saw king and his servants crossing toward him. Araunah went out and bowed to king, his face to ground.
Araunah said, Why has my lord king come to his servant? David said, To buy threshing floor from you, to build altar to YHWH, so plague may be restrained from people.
Araunah said to David, Let my lord king take and offer what is good in his eyes. Look, cattle for burnt offering, and threshing sledges and equipment/yokes of cattle for wood.
All this Araunah, O king, gives to king. Araunah said to king, May YHWH your Divine accept you.

King said to Araunah, No, but buying, I will buy it from you for a price. I will not offer to YHWH my Divine burnt offerings that cost me nothing. David bought threshing floor and cattle for fifty silver shekels.
David built there an altar to YHWH and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. YHWH was entreated for land, and plague was restrained from Israel.

translation_notes: Verse 1 attributes the incitement to YHWH, while 1 Chronicles 21 attributes it to an adversary/Satan. The contradiction must remain visible and prevents a single simplistic doctrine of Divine causation.
Counting people is not inherently sinful. In context the census concerns military capacity, royal reach, conscription, taxation, and extraction; Joab's objection suggests the king's purpose matters.
The totals differ from Chronicles and are logistically difficult. Ancient census figures are theological and political data, not precise modern statistics.
David confesses after the count, but the offered consequences fall primarily upon the population. A ruler's sin must not ethically authorize punishment of civilians.
The famine option reads seven years in the Masoretic Text and three in several ancient witnesses and Chronicles. The variant must be preserved.
David chooses based on Divine mercy, yet seventy thousand other people die. The narrative's collective-punishment theology is morally difficult and cannot become a model for justice.
Plague language must never be used to claim modern epidemics are Divine punishment or that victims deserve disease.
YHWH relents and stops the angel, which places mercy and cessation inside the story rather than treating maximal destruction as inevitable.
David finally names command responsibility: he sinned and the people did not. Calling them sheep reflects royal paternalism; they remain persons, households, and communities.
Araunah is Jebusite, a member of the population whose city David conquered. His offer occurs before a king accompanied by servants during emergency, creating severe power imbalance.
David's payment matters, but later temple and land traditions may not use this purchase to justify displacement, conquest, or modern territorial claims.
Chronicles gives a different price—six hundred gold shekels—for the place. The land-price traditions must not be harmonized invisibly.
Animals are killed in sacrifice. Plague cessation must not be interpreted as proof that blood payment purchases Divine forgiveness.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Do not blame epidemics on sin, punish populations for rulers, or treat death totals as acceptable collateral for spiritual lessons. Do not use Araunah's emergency transaction for land entitlement. Center Joab's objection, civilian deaths, Divine relenting, David's admission of responsibility, and the urgent stopping of harm.
